data: A time management and productivity application
An app to learn mobile development and manage my goals
A realtime, multiplayer Connect Four game
A puzzle-platformer video game with magnets as its core mechanic.
Leading lab sections, holding office hours, and helping students learn the Right Way™

Using ReasonML/Ocaml to extend the language with novel new features. What I have worked on (or am currently worked) on is: 
 - Implemented a novel algorithm for examples for inexhaustive pattern matching when inconsistencies are present (a challenging "starter project"!).
 - Developing a novel algorithm to handle (cyclic!) constraint graphs to provide the programmer all possible types for all holes in their program

Leading lab sections, holding office hours, and actively seeking to improve existing course material (e.g. I redid our lab slides on DP with 100+ new slides because the existing ones presented a single problem that was too hard for most students to understand.)
I collaborate with other developers to create interesting real world projects, such as a Fintech app, and an app for keeping track of your friend group's incredible Lore.
I worked with MDining to identify areas where we could reduce palm oil consumption. To do this, I developed a Python script to estimate palm oil from thousands of product's ingredient list.

I IA for this course! I learned quite a bit about concurrency, networking, and especially virtual memory. However, I learned a lot more about debugging. A lot about debugging, especially the incredible value of spamming as many assertions as humanly possible + fuzzing.
A fun continuation of EECS 483. Learning LLVM was cool, and partially vibecoding the final project was also fun.
I think the course was interesting from the perspective of learning a bit about how to program GPUs in CUDA, but the course structure and teaching left much to be desired in my opinion.

I developed a compiler over the semester to go from a high-level, functional programming language to SSA, and then to Assembly. Conceptually heavy at times, but a really fun and interesting class that has furthered my interest in this field.

The first half was familiar to me, but I still learned some new things and enjoyed it. I took a lot of interest in the distributed systems/MapReduce in the second half, and have since wanted to explore this area more.

An infinite but fun time sink during an already busy semester, but a rewarding and fun class. I'm really proud of the games we made, and I learned quite a bit about game development. One of the most important takeaways for me though was thinking about how to guide users and the value of play testing sessions. Both are quite practical for building tech in the real world!
I really enjoyed the content of this class, especially algorithms and reducations, but the class was nevertheless quite challenging for me.

Projects were pretty interesting, especially the linker. This course helped me understand a bit more about what's going on inside a computer. Three main takeaways: branch prediction, struct alignment, and caching solves all problems.
I IAed for this course for several semesters! Speedrunning the projects was pretty fun, and the course content is fairly interesting and useful.
Cool class; I definitely learned things about Git, and it carved the way for me to become an Nvim enjoyer
